represents [All-Ireland](/wiki/All-Ireland "All-Ireland") in international cricket. The Irish Cricket Union, operating under the brand [Cricket Ireland](/wiki/Cricket_Ireland "Cricket Ireland"), is the sport's governing body in Ireland, and they organise the international team. The team have a number of home grounds, including [Malahide](/wiki/Malahide_Cricket_Club "Malahide Cricket Club") in County Dublin, [Stormont, Belfast](/wiki/Stormont_(cricket_ground) "Stormont (cricket ground)"), [Bready](/wiki/Bready_Cricket_Club_Ground "Bready Cricket Club Ground") in the north-west and [Clontarf](/wiki/Clontarf_Cricket_Club "Clontarf Cricket Club") in Dublin city. A further ground is planned for the [National Sports Campus](/wiki/National_Sports_Campus "National Sports Campus") in Abbotstown, Dublin for 2030.[[10]](#cite_note-11) Due to the short season allowed by the Irish climate, and lack of large scale facilities, Ireland also occasionally play 'home matches' in venues in England and further abroad.

The **Ireland men's cricket team** represents [All-Ireland](/wiki/All-Ireland "All-Ireland") in international cricket. The Irish Cricket Union, operating under the brand [Cricket Ireland](/wiki/Cricket_Ireland "Cricket Ireland"), is the sport's governing body in Ireland, and they organise the international team. The team have a number of home grounds, including [Malahide](/wiki/Malahide_Cricket_Club "Malahide Cricket Club") in County Dublin, [Stormont, Belfast](/wiki/Stormont_(cricket_ground) "Stormont (cricket ground)"), [Bready](/wiki/Bready_Cricket_Club_Ground "Bready Cricket Club Ground") in the north-west and [Clontarf](/wiki/Clontarf_Cricket_Club "Clontarf Cricket Club") in Dublin city. A further ground is planned for the [National Sports Campus](/wiki/National_Sports_Campus "National Sports Campus") in Abbotstown, Dublin for 2030.[[10]](#cite_note-11) Due to the short season allowed by the Irish climate, and lack of large scale facilities, Ireland also occasionally play 'home matches' in venues in England and further abroad.

Ireland's men participate in all three major forms of the international game, [Test](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket"), [One-Day International](/wiki/One-Day_International "One-Day International") (ODI) and [Twenty20 International](/wiki/Twenty20_International "Twenty20 International") (T20I) matches. They are the 11th [Full Member](/wiki/List_of_International_Cricket_Council_members#Full_Members "List of International Cricket Council members") of the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (ICC), and the second Full Member from Europe, having been awarded [Test status](/wiki/Test_cricket#Test_status "Test cricket"), along with [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan_national_cricket_team "Afghanistan national cricket team"), on 22 June 2017.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) Ireland and Afghanistan are the most recently appointed Test-playing nations.

Modern Cricket was introduced to Ireland in the 19th century, though cricket may have partly evolved from earlier Gaelic games invented in Ireland.[[15]](#cite_note-irishtimes.com-16)[[16]](#cite_note-OffThe_Ball-17)[[17]](#cite_note-BRIEF_HISTORY_OF_IRISH_CRICKET-18)[[18]](#cite_note-Cricket_on_solid_local_footing-19) The first match played by an Ireland team was in 1855. Ireland toured Canada and the United States in the late 19th century, and occasionally hosted matches against touring sides. Ireland's most significant early international rivalry, with the [Scotland national cricket team](/wiki/Scotland_national_cricket_team "Scotland national cricket team"), was established when the teams first played each other in 1888.[[19]](#cite_note-20) Ireland's maiden [first-class](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") match was played in 1902.

Ireland were elected to Associate membership of the ICC in 1993, but played their first full ODI in 2006 against [England](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team") in the build-up to the [2007 ICC Cricket World Cup](/wiki/2007_ICC_Cricket_World_Cup "2007 ICC Cricket World Cup"), their first successful qualification. At that tournament, a series of eye-catching results against Full Members, including a draw against Zimbabwe, and wins against Pakistan and Bangladesh confirmed Ireland's ODI status after the competition. Since then, they have gone on to play 197 ODIs, resulting in 78 victories, 101 defeats, 15 [no results](/wiki/Result_(cricket)#No_result "Result (cricket)"), and 3 [ties](/wiki/Tie_(draw) "Tie (draw)").[[20]](#cite_note-odiresults-21) Contracts for players were introduced in 2009, marking the transition to becoming a professional team.

Further success in the shortest format meant the Ireland team also qualified for the 2009, 2010, 2012, 2014, 2016, 2021, 2022,[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23) and 2024[[b]](#cite_note-24) [World Twenty20](/wiki/ICC_World_Twenty20 "ICC World Twenty20") competitions, and have qualified for the future [2026 T20 World Cup](/wiki/2026_ICC_Men%27s_T20_World_Cup "2026 ICC Men's T20 World Cup") by [ICC men's T20 world rankings](/wiki/Cricket_World_Rankings "Cricket World Rankings").

Before attaining Test status, Ireland also played first-class international cricket in the [ICC Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "ICC Intercontinental Cup"), which they have won four times between 2005 and 2013. Due to their successes in the first-class [ICC Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "ICC Intercontinental Cup") competition, and further high-profile wins at the World Cups of [2011](/wiki/2011_ICC_Cricket_World_Cup "2011 ICC Cricket World Cup") (against England) and [2015](/wiki/2015_ICC_Cricket_World_Cup "2015 ICC Cricket World Cup") (against West Indies and Zimbabwe), they were labelled the "leading Associate"[[23]](#cite_note-25) and stated their intention to become a full member by 2020. This intention was realised in June 2017, when the ICC unanimously decided to award Ireland and Afghanistan Full Member status, which allows them to participate in Test matches.[[24]](#cite_note-26) Ireland's first test was a home fixture at The Village, Malahide between 11–15 May 2018 against [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan_national_cricket_team "Pakistan national cricket team"), and they have played tests somewhat sporadically since. Their first test victory was against fellow new boys [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan_national_cricket_team "Afghanistan national cricket team") in Sharjah on 12 March 2024.

Ireland remain the only test playing nation, where the [women's team](/wiki/Ireland_women%27s_cricket_team "Ireland women's cricket team") have played a test match before the men's team.[[25]](#cite_note-27) Ireland was a women's test nation seventeen years before the men, playing their only test also against Pakistan.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ireland_cricket_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ireland_cricket_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/All-Ireland_United_Cricket_team_at_Phoenix_Park%2C_Dublin%2C_ca._1858.jpg/250px-All-Ireland_United_Cricket_team_at_Phoenix_Park%2C_Dublin%2C_ca._1858.jpg)](/wiki/File:All-Ireland_United_Cricket_team_at_Phoenix_Park,_Dublin,_ca._1858.jpg)

All-Ireland United Cricket team at Phoenix Park, Dublin, ca. 1858

The first mention of modern cricket in Ireland was in 1731 when the Military of Ireland and the Gentlemen of Ireland took each other on in the [Phoenix Park](/wiki/Phoenix_Park "Phoenix Park"), where the game still thrives in one of the world's oldest established cricket clubs. However, the origins of Cricket may, in fact, have come from Ireland, via the ancient Gaelic game of 'Katty', a precursor to the modern game of [Rounders](/wiki/Rounders "Rounders") in Ireland. Katty was exported around the world by Irishmen serving in the British Army and the game was adopted as both a training and recreational activity by British Army soldiers. The evolution of Katty in the empire is reflected in the use of anglicised Gaelic terms such as crioc, crios, bail, googly, still used in cricket as we know it today.[[15]](#cite_note-irishtimes.com-16)[[16]](#cite_note-OffThe_Ball-17)[[17]](#cite_note-BRIEF_HISTORY_OF_IRISH_CRICKET-18)[[18]](#cite_note-Cricket_on_solid_local_footing-19)

Modern Cricket was introduced to Ireland by the English in the towns of [Kilkenny](/wiki/Kilkenny "Kilkenny") and [Ballinasloe](/wiki/Ballinasloe "Ballinasloe") in the early 19th century. In the 1830s, the game began to spread; many of the clubs which were founded in the following 30 years are still in existence today.[[26]](#cite_note-cricinfo_history-28) The first Irish national team played in 1855 against The Gentlemen of England in Dublin. In the 1850s, the Englishman [Charles Lawrence](/wiki/Charles_Lawrence_(cricketer) "Charles Lawrence (cricketer)") was responsible for developing the game in Ireland through his coaching.[[26]](#cite_note-cricinfo_history-28) In the 1850s and 1860s, Ireland was visited for the first time by touring professional teams. Ireland's first match against [Marylebone Cricket Club](/wiki/Marylebone_Cricket_Club "Marylebone Cricket Club") (the M.C.C.) was in 1858.[[26]](#cite_note-cricinfo_history-28)[[16]](#cite_note-OffThe_Ball-17)

Cricket had flourished throughout the Ireland until the early 1880s. However, the Irish [Land War](/wiki/Land_War "Land War"), the [Gaelic Revival](/wiki/Gaelic_Revival "Gaelic Revival") cultural movement and its offshoot the [Gaelic Athletic Association](/wiki/Gaelic_Athletic_Association "Gaelic Athletic Association"), founded in 1884, began to challenge the popularity of cricket.[[27]](#cite_note-29) There were widespread calls to establish a national cricketing union in Ireland the 1870s to oversee the sport but landlords who owned the estates on which cricket was predominantly played declined to do so, many believing the game would distract their tenant farmers from working and thus impact the estates' income at a time when the [land war](/wiki/Land_War "Land War") was already depleting landlords wealth. One of those to call for a formal body to organise cricket was [Michael Cusack](/wiki/Michael_Cusack_(Gaelic_Athletic_Association) "Michael Cusack (Gaelic Athletic Association)"), later the founder of the GAA. Cusack, was a keen cricketer, later tried to organise cricket under the umbrella of the newly formed Gaelic Athletic Association. His motion, however, lost out narrowly. Consequently, in 1902 cricket fell under the GAA's newly introduced Law 27 banning its members from playing "foreign", in practice, British, games. Until the ban was lifted in 1970, anyone playing foreign games, such as cricket or [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), was banned from the membership of the GAA, the organisation responsible for games such as [hurling](/wiki/Hurling "Hurling"), [Gaelic football](/wiki/Gaelic_football "Gaelic football"), [rounders](/wiki/Rounders "Rounders"), and [Gaelic handball](/wiki/Gaelic_handball "Gaelic handball").[[28]](#cite_note-30)

Thereafter Cricket in Ireland declined in popularity, especially in the era after the [land war](/wiki/Land_War "Land War") as the sport became associated with being a "garrison game" of the British Army. Participation became increasingly restricted to Catholic and Protestant public schools, the Anglo-Irish population, and unionists. Irish teams toured Canada and the US in 1879, 1888, 1892, and 1909. On top of this, Ireland defeated a touring South African side in 1904.[[26]](#cite_note-cricinfo_history-28) Their first match with [first-class](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") status was played on 19 May 1902 against a London County side including [W.G. Grace](/wiki/W.G._Grace "W.G. Grace"). The Irish, captained by [Sir Tim O'Brien](/wiki/Sir_Tim_O%27Brien,_3rd_Baronet "Sir Tim O'Brien, 3rd Baronet"), won convincingly by [238 runs](/wiki/The_result_in_cricket "The result in cricket").[[29]](#cite_note-31) An Irish Cricket union was eventually set up in 1923 but some clubs refused to recognise its legitimacy. It was only in 2001 that Cricket Ireland became the official oversight body for cricket in Ireland recognised by all cricket clubs on the island.[[15]](#cite_note-irishtimes.com-16)[[16]](#cite_note-OffThe_Ball-17)[[17]](#cite_note-BRIEF_HISTORY_OF_IRISH_CRICKET-18)[[18]](#cite_note-Cricket_on_solid_local_footing-19)

### Before 1993

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ireland_cricket_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Before 1993")]

After the 1902 tour of England, where four matches yielded one win, two draws and one loss, Ireland did not play first-class cricket again for five years.[[30]](#cite_note-32) Although the team had lost to the South Africans in 1894 – Ireland's first match against a Test-playing nation – Ireland defeated South Africa in 1904; it was the team's first victory against a Test side.[[31]](#cite_note-Irish_timeline-33) In 1909, the first annual first-class match between Ireland and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_cricket_team "Scotland cricket team") was held, and an annual match against the [MCC](/wiki/Marylebone_Cricket_Club "Marylebone Cricket Club") was arranged from 1924 onwards.[[26]](#cite_note-cricinfo_history-28)

The Irish played yearly first-class matches with the Scots, only interrupted by world wars, until 1999, but all their other cricket depended upon touring international sides finding it convenient to include a visit to Ireland in their schedules. However, Ireland sometimes surprised [Test](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") nations on these occasions, beating the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indian_cricket_team "West Indian cricket team") by 60 runs in a three-day match in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin") in 1928, for example; it was Ireland's first match against the West Indies.[[31]](#cite_note-Irish_timeline-33) In 1969, in a match played at [Sion Mills](/wiki/Sion_Mills "Sion Mills") in [County Tyrone](/wiki/County_Tyrone "County Tyrone"), the team defeated a West Indian side including [Clive Lloyd](/wiki/Clive_Lloyd "Clive Lloyd") and [Clyde Walcott](/wiki/Clyde_Walcott "Clyde Walcott") by nine wickets, after bowling them out for 25.[[32]](#cite_note-34) This was the last time Ireland defeated a touring side until 2003, when they beat [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwean_cricket_team "Zimbabwean cricket team") by ten wickets.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

The Scots and the Irish were mostly competing with [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_cricket_team "Sri Lankan cricket team") for the title as the best non-Test nation at the time – indeed, Ireland drew with Sri Lanka in a rain-hit first-class match in 1979, Ireland scoring a total of 341 for 7 in two innings, while Sri Lanka made 288 for 6 in one innings. Ireland, along with Scotland and the Netherlands, has at times played in competitions for [English county cricket sides](/wiki/List_of_English_First_Class_Cricket_Counties "List of English First Class Cricket Counties"), including the [Benson & Hedges Cup](/wiki/Benson_%26_Hedges_Cup "Benson & Hedges Cup") and the [Friends Provident Trophy](/wiki/Friends_Provident_Trophy "Friends Provident Trophy") (previously the [C&G Trophy](/wiki/C%26G_Trophy "C&G Trophy")). Since there is no nationality restriction in county cricket, non-Irish people were allowed to compete for Ireland in these matches. For example, [Hansie Cronje](/wiki/Hansie_Cronje "Hansie Cronje") of South Africa played for Ireland in 1997,[[34]](#cite_note-36) as did New Zealander [Jesse Ryder](/wiki/Jesse_Ryder "Jesse Ryder") in 2007.[[35]](#cite_note-37)

### Associate Member (1993–2007)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ireland_cricket_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Associate Member (1993–2007)")]

Ireland joined the ICC as an [Associate Member](/wiki/List_of_International_Cricket_Council_members#Associate_Members "List of International Cricket Council members") in 1993, a year before Scotland.[[36]](#cite_note-38) This meant Ireland could play in the [ICC Trophy](/wiki/ICC_Trophy "ICC Trophy") for the first time in 1994, and they finished seventh in the tournament.[[37]](#cite_note-39) Three years later they progressed to the semi-finals of the competition but lost the third place play-off with Scotland, thus missing a place at the [1999 cricket World Cup](/wiki/1999_cricket_World_Cup "1999 cricket World Cup"). Ireland finished eighth in the 2001 tournament.[[38]](#cite_note-40) After this, [Adrian Birrell](/wiki/Adrian_Birrell "Adrian Birrell") was hired as coach.[[17]](#cite_note-BRIEF_HISTORY_OF_IRISH_CRICKET-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Ireland_compete_against_Essex_at_Castle_Avenue%2C_Dublin%2C_13_May_2007%2C_Friends_Provident_Trophy_-_100_1795_%282%29.jpg/250px-Ireland_compete_against_Essex_at_Castle_Avenue%2C_Dublin%2C_13_May_2007%2C_Friends_Provident_Trophy_-_100_1795_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ireland_compete_against_Essex_at_Castle_Avenue,_Dublin,_13_May_2007,_Friends_Provident_Trophy_-_100_1795_(2).jpg)

Ireland playing against [Essex](/wiki/Essex_County_Cricket_Club "Essex County Cricket Club") in the [Friends Provident Trophy](/wiki/Friends_Provident_Trophy "Friends Provident Trophy") at [Clontarf](/wiki/Clontarf_Cricket_Club_Ground "Clontarf Cricket Club Ground") in 2007.

With the introduction of the [ICC Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "ICC Intercontinental Cup") in 2004,[[39]](#cite_note-41) Ireland had a chance to play first-class on a regular basis. After failing to progress beyond the group stages in the 2004 [competition](/wiki/2004_ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "2004 ICC Intercontinental Cup"),[[40]](#cite_note-42) Ireland won their first Cup title in [October 2005](/wiki/2005_ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "2005 ICC Intercontinental Cup") with a six-wicket win over Kenya.[[41]](#cite_note-43) The [2005 ICC Trophy](/wiki/2005_ICC_Trophy "2005 ICC Trophy"), which was hosted in Ireland – the group stages in [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast"), Northern Ireland, the final stages in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") – saw the Irish make the final, which they lost to Scotland.[[42]](#cite_note-44) Though Ireland were runners-up, they had secured their place at the 2007 World Cup as well as an extra $500,000 over the next four years from the ICC to encourage development of Irish cricket.[[43]](#cite_note-Edward_Craig-45) They also gained official ODI status.[[43]](#cite_note-Edward_Craig-45)

Ireland's inaugural ODI was played in front of a full house of 7,500 spectators at [Stormont](/wiki/Stormont_(cricket_ground) "Stormont (cricket ground)"), [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast"), on 13 June 2006 against England. It was the first time Ireland had played the full England side. Though Ireland lost by 38 runs, they were praised by [Andrew Strauss](/wiki/Andrew_Strauss "Andrew Strauss"), England's stand-in captain.[[44]](#cite_note-46)[[45]](#cite_note-47)

August saw them participate in Division One of the European Championship, against Denmark, [Italy](/wiki/Italian_cricket_team "Italian cricket team"), the Netherlands and Scotland. The games against the Netherlands and Scotland had ODI status. In the tournament, and what was the team's second ODI, Ireland recorded their first ODI win, beating fellow Associates Scotland by 85 runs after man-of-the-match Eoin Morgan made 99.[[47]](#cite_note-49) Although the match against the Netherlands was a no-result, Ireland won the European Championship title.[[48]](#cite_note-50) Ireland's second Intercontinental Cup title came in the [2006–2007 competition](/wiki/ICC_Intercontinental_Cup#2006–2007_ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "ICC Intercontinental Cup"). They faced Canada in the final and won by an innings and 115 runs, the four-day match concluding within two days. This made Ireland the first team to successfully defend the Continental Cup.[[49]](#cite_note-51)

For the 2006 season, the C&G Trophy was reorganised to include a round-robin stage instead of being entirely knock-out. Whereas Ireland had only one match guaranteed in the tournament before, they now had more fixtures against English county sides. Ireland recorded one win in their nine matches.[[50]](#cite_note-52) Ireland participated in the competition until it was restructured again in 2009. In that time they played 25 matches and won two.[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[52]](#cite_note-54) The latter of those victories was against [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire_County_Cricket_Club "Worcestershire County Cricket Club"); in that match Ireland bowled Worcestershire out for 58, which was their lowest ever one-day total. It was the first time that Ireland had bowled out a county for less than 100.[[53]](#cite_note-55)[[54]](#cite_note-56) Ireland were invited to participate in the reformatted competition from 2010 onwards, but chose not to do so, and instead focused their limited financial resources on international cricket.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

### One-Day International status without test status (2007–2016)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ireland_cricket_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: One-Day International status without test status (2007–2016)")]

At the start of 2007, Ireland saw more than three months of almost constant cricket. First was a visit to Kenya, where they took part in [Division One](/wiki/2007_ICC_World_Cricket_League_Division_One "2007 ICC World Cricket League Division One") of the [ICC World Cricket League](/wiki/ICC_World_Cricket_League "ICC World Cricket League"). They finished fifth in the league after four narrow defeats, and Kenya won the league.[[56]](#cite_note-58) Before the World Cup, the team participated in a high-performance camp in South Africa.[[57]](#cite_note-tough_at_the_top-59) Ireland's performance in their inaugural World Cup in the [2007 Cricket World Cup](/wiki/Ireland_at_the_Cricket_World_Cup "Ireland at the Cricket World Cup") took many pundits by surprise. In their first game, on 15 March, they tied with [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwean_cricket_team "Zimbabwean cricket team"), primarily thanks to Ireland's first ever World Cup century by man-of-the-match [Jeremy Bray](/wiki/Jeremy_Bray_(cricketer) "Jeremy Bray (cricketer)") and economical bowling in the final overs by Trent Johnston and Andre Botha.[[58]](#cite_note-60) In their second match, played on [Saint Patrick's Day](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Day "Saint Patrick's Day"), they beat the fourth-ranked team in the world, [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistani_cricket_team "Pakistani cricket team"), by three wickets, thus knocking Pakistan out of the competition.[[59]](#cite_note-61)

These two results were enough to advance Ireland to the Super-8 stage of the tournament. In their final group-stage game, the West Indies beat them by eight wickets.[[61]](#cite_note-63) In the Super 8 stage, they lost their matches against England, South Africa, New Zealand, Australia, and Sri Lanka, but recorded a 74-run victory against Test-playing nation [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh_national_cricket_team "Bangladesh national cricket team"), the 9th-ranked team in the world. The team received a heroes' welcome in Dublin.[[62]](#cite_note-64)

After the World Cup, former West Indies cricketer [Phil Simmons](/wiki/Phil_Simmons "Phil Simmons") took over the role of coach from Birrell.[[63]](#cite_note-65) [India](/wiki/Indian_cricket_team "Indian cricket team") were scheduled to play [South Africa](/wiki/South_African_cricket_team "South African cricket team") in a series of One Day Internationals in Ireland in June 2007. Ireland also played one-off matches at Stormont against the two teams. Missing several players from their World Cup squad, Ireland lost both games.[[64]](#cite_note-66) Ireland hosted a [quadrangular tournament in Dublin and Belfast](/wiki/Quadrangular_Series_in_Ireland_in_2007 "Quadrangular Series in Ireland in 2007") in July involving the West Indies, the Netherlands, and Scotland. Ireland and the West Indies both won their games against Scotland and the Netherlands with their direct encounter ending in [no result](/wiki/The_result_in_cricket#No_result "The result in cricket") due to rain. The West Indies won the tournament because of a bonus point won against the Netherlands.[[65]](#cite_note-67) Trent Johnston stepped down as captain and was replaced by [William Porterfield](/wiki/William_Porterfield "William Porterfield") in March 2008.[[66]](#cite_note-68)

The [2007–08 ICC Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "2007–08 ICC Intercontinental Cup") began in June, with Ireland playing their first match in August. In November 2008, the team's campaign ended. After finishing second in the round-robin stage of the competition, Ireland faced [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia_cricket_team "Namibia cricket team") in the final. Ireland won by nine wickets, securing their third consecutive Intercontinental Cup title.[[67]](#cite_note-69) In March 2008 Ireland toured Bangladesh, playing three ODIs against the hosts and losing all of them.[[68]](#cite_note-70) In July, Ireland played a [tri-series](/wiki/2008_Associates_Tri-Series_in_Scotland "2008 Associates Tri-Series in Scotland") against New Zealand and Scotland in [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen "Aberdeen") but lost both matches.[[69]](#cite_note-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Ireland_vs_Pakistan2_cropped.jpg/250px-Ireland_vs_Pakistan2_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ireland_vs_Pakistan2_cropped.jpg)

Ireland playing against Pakistan at the Kennington Oval during the [2009 T20 World Cup](/wiki/2009_ICC_World_Twenty20 "2009 ICC World Twenty20"). [Niall O'Brien](/wiki/Niall_O%27Brien_(cricketer) "Niall O'Brien (cricketer)") is keeping wicket whilst and [Trent Johnston](/wiki/Trent_Johnston "Trent Johnston") is the fielder.

Reigning champions Ireland hosted the [European Cricket Championship](/wiki/European_Cricket_Championship "European Cricket Championship") (Division One) in late July and they won their third European title, winning every match, including the decisive encounter against Scotland by seven wickets.[[70]](#cite_note-72) In early August, Ireland hosted five other Associate nations at the [2009 ICC World Twenty20 Qualifier](/wiki/2009_ICC_World_Twenty20_Qualifier "2009 ICC World Twenty20 Qualifier") in Belfast; this was Ireland's [Twenty20 International](/wiki/Twenty20_International "Twenty20 International") debut. Ireland would have faced the Netherlands in the final, however the match was rained off and the teams shared the trophy.[[71]](#cite_note-73) By getting to the final of the tournament, Ireland qualified for the [2009 ICC World Twenty20](/wiki/2009_ICC_World_Twenty20 "2009 ICC World Twenty20") in England in June 2009. Later in August, Ireland were due to play three ODIs at home against Kenya. Ireland won the first game, the second game could not be finished due to rain and the last match was completely washed out.[[72]](#cite_note-74) In October, the team visited Kenya for a [tri-series of ODIs](/wiki/2008_Associates_Tri-Series_in_Kenya "2008 Associates Tri-Series in Kenya") with the hosts and Zimbabwe. Only two of Ireland's four games in the round-robin stage could be played, the others were rained off. Ireland lost their first match to Zimbabwe,[[73]](#cite_note-75) but won their second against Kenya, though they failed to qualify for the final.[[74]](#cite_note-76)[[75]](#cite_note-77)

In the run-up to the 2009 ICC World Twenty20, Ireland were deprived of batsman [Eoin Morgan](/wiki/Eoin_Morgan "Eoin Morgan"), similarly to [Ed Joyce](/wiki/Ed_Joyce "Ed Joyce") several years earlier, who was selected to play for England, making him ineligible to play for Ireland again.[[76]](#cite_note-78) Ireland played their first Twenty20 International against a full ICC member side on 8 June 2009 and in their opening match of the tournament defeated Bangladesh by four wickets and knocked them out of the tournament.[[77]](#cite_note-:0-79)[[78]](#cite_note-80) Ireland progressed to the second stage of the competition. They were grouped with [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_cricket_team "New Zealand national cricket team"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan_national_cricket_team "Pakistan national cricket team") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka_national_cricket_team "Sri Lanka national cricket team") and lost all three of their matches. In 2009, Ireland played nine ODIs, winning the seven they played against Associate nations, losing their only match against a Test team (England), and one match was abandoned.[[79]](#cite_note-81)

Ireland played 17 One Day Internationals in 2010, winning 11 (including a victory over Bangladesh) and losing six.[[80]](#cite_note-82) Ireland were knocked out of the [2010 ICC World Twenty20](/wiki/2010_ICC_World_Twenty20 "2010 ICC World Twenty20"), hosted by South Africa in April and May, after being beaten by the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies_cricket_team "West Indies cricket team") and a washed out match against England.

The [2011 Cricket World Cup](/wiki/Ireland_at_the_Cricket_World_Cup "Ireland at the Cricket World Cup") was held between February and March and hosted by Bangladesh, India and Sri Lanka. Though Ireland did not progress beyond the first round they secured a historic victory against England.[[81]](#cite_note-2011review-83) Ireland beat England by 3 wickets with [Kevin O'Brien](/wiki/Kevin_O%27Brien_(cricketer) "Kevin O'Brien (cricketer)") hitting the fastest century in World Cup history, managing the feat in just 50 balls.[[82]](#cite_note-84) In passing England's total of 327 for victory, Ireland broke the record for the highest successful run chase in the World Cup.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

### Test status (2017–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ireland_cricket_team&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Test status (2017–present)")]

In January 2012 Cricket Ireland chief executive Warren Deutrom publicly declared Ireland's ambition to play [Test cricket](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") by 2020. Their desire to achieve Test status was in part to stem the tide of Irish players using residency rules to switch to England for the opportunity to play Test cricket. Deutrom outlined the ambition as he unveiled the new strategic plan for Irish cricket to 2015. The plan set out a series of stretching goals including increasing the number of participants in the game to 50,000, setting a target of reaching 8th in the World rankings, establishing a domestic first-class cricket structure, and reinforcing cricket as the fifth most popular team sport in Ireland.[[92]](#cite_note-CricketIreland2012a-94)[[93]](#cite_note-BBC2012a-95)

Deutrom had already sent a letter to the ICC in 2009 stating his board's intention to apply for Full Membership – a potential pathway to Test cricket – and to seek clarification on the process.[[94]](#cite_note-96) Former Australian bowler [Jason Gillespie](/wiki/Jason_Gillespie "Jason Gillespie") said that if Ireland got Test status it "would be huge news in world cricket, and it would be a massive positive story for the world game".[[95]](#cite_note-Gillespie-97) Following Ireland's victory over the West Indies in the [2015 Cricket World Cup](/wiki/2015_Cricket_World_Cup "2015 Cricket World Cup"), former fast bowler [Michael Holding](/wiki/Michael_Holding "Michael Holding") said that the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") should grant Ireland Test status immediately, saying "they need to be recognised now".[[96]](#cite_note-Holding-98) The ICC said in 2015 that Ireland would be granted Test status in 2019 should they win the [2015–17 ICC Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9317_ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "2015–17 ICC Intercontinental Cup") and beat the 10th ranked Test nation in a four-match Test series in 2018.[[97]](#cite_note-99)

However, on 22 June 2017, after more than a decade of playing top-class international cricket, full ICC membership was granted to Ireland (along with Afghanistan) at an ICC meeting in London, thus making them the eleventh Test cricket team.[[98]](#cite_note-100) In October 2017, the ICC announced that Ireland's first Test match would be at home [against Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistani_cricket_team_in_Ireland_in_2018 "Pakistani cricket team in Ireland in 2018") in May 2018.[[99]](#cite_note-confirm-101)[[100]](#cite_note-CI1st-102) Ireland played their first 'touring' Test in India in March 2019 against fellow newcomers [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan_national_cricket_team "Afghanistan national cricket team"), where they lost by 7 wickets. This was followed by a four-day Test match against England at [Lord's](/wiki/Lord%27s "Lord's") [in July 2019](/wiki/Irish_cricket_team_in_England_in_2019 "Irish cricket team in England in 2019").[[101]](#cite_note-Lord's-103) According to the [ICC Future Tours Programme](/wiki/ICC_Future_Tours_Programme "ICC Future Tours Programme") for 2019–23, Ireland are scheduled to play sixteen Tests, but along with Afghanistan and Zimbabwe, are not included in the first two editions of the [ICC World Test Championship](/wiki/World_Test_Championship "World Test Championship").[[102]](#cite_note-IreFTP-104)

Ireland played England in a four-day Test match at Lord's in July 2019. Even though they bowled England out for 85 before lunch on the first day, they were themselves all out for 38 in the final innings, losing the match by 143 runs.[[103]](#cite_note-106)

In October 2019, [Andrew Balbirnie](/wiki/Andrew_Balbirnie "Andrew Balbirnie") was appointed Test and ODI captain and one month later, in November he also took over the captaincy of T20I from [Gary Wilson](/wiki/Gary_Wilson_(cricketer) "Gary Wilson (cricketer)"), thus becoming all format captain for Ireland.[[104]](#cite_note-107)

On 16 January 2022, Ireland claimed their first away ODI series win over a fellow Test nation by beating the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies_cricket "West Indies cricket") by two wickets at [Sabina Park](/wiki/Sabina_Park "Sabina Park") in [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica").[[105]](#cite_note-Away_ODI_series_win-108) On 31 March 2023, Ireland claimed their first win against Bangladesh on Bangladeshi soil in the third match of their T20 three match series.[[106]](#cite_note-Away_Bangladesh_win-109) Ireland achieved their first-ever series win in Zimbabwe, after winning a three-match series 2–1 in December 2023.[[107]](#cite_note-110) The following week they followed up their T20 success with a 2–0 one-day international series victory, which was their first ODI series win in Zimbabwe.[[108]](#cite_note-111)

Ireland won their first Test on 1 March 2024 (after losing their first seven), defeating Afghanistan in Abu Dhabi by six wickets.[[109]](#cite_note-112)

A couple months after their first Test victory, Ireland secured their first T20 victory over Pakistan in two attempts, winning by five wickets with one ball remaining.[[111]](#cite_note-115) In July 2024 Ireland beat Zimbabwe by four wickets at [Stormont](/wiki/Stormont_(cricket_ground) "Stormont (cricket ground)") and recorded a second consecutive Test victory and a first on home soil.[[112]](#cite_note-116) In September 2024 [Ross Adair](/wiki/Ross_Adair "Ross Adair")'s maiden century powered Ireland to their first T20 win over South Africa to share the series 1–1.[[113]](#cite_note-117) Ireland followed this up with a 69-run victory over South Africa in the final match of their ODI series, their second ever victory over South Africa in the ODI format.[[114]](#cite_note-118)
In May 2025 Ireland beat the West Indies by 124 runs in the first ODI in Dublin marking, its biggest win by runs against a Full Member and their fourth-biggest victory overall.[[115]](#cite_note-119)

## Governing body

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ireland_cricket_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Governing body")]

Main article: [Irish Cricket Union](/wiki/Irish_Cricket_Union "Irish Cricket Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Cricket_Ireland_flag.svg/250px-Cricket_Ireland_flag.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cricket_Ireland_flag.svg)

Flag of the Irish team

The [Irish Cricket Union](/wiki/Irish_Cricket_Union "Irish Cricket Union") (ICU) – the governing body of Irish cricket – was officially founded in 1923, although its predecessor had been active since 1890.[[26]](#cite_note-cricinfo_history-28)

In common with a number of other Ireland sporting governing bodies, the Union was formed to represent cricket throughout the island of Ireland, rather than just the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"). In common with its counterparts for [rugby union](/wiki/Irish_Rugby_Football_Union "Irish Rugby Football Union"), [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_League_Ireland "Rugby League Ireland") and [field hockey](/wiki/Irish_Hockey_Association "Irish Hockey Association"), the Union therefore does not use the [Irish tricolor](/wiki/Irish_tricolor "Irish tricolor"), but instead employs its own flag, which is used by such bodies as the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") to represent the team and in ICC tournaments; "[Ireland's Call](/wiki/Ireland%27s_Call "Ireland's Call")" is used as the national anthem.[[125]](#cite_note-129)[[126]](#cite_note-130)

After the World Cup, Irish cricket had poor results in the 2007 Friends Provident Trophy, as many players were unavailable. The Irish cricket team was an amateur side and most of the players had full-time jobs with commitments conflicting with cricket.[[57]](#cite_note-tough_at_the_top-59)

Warren Deutrom, the chief executive of the ICU, has stated that it wants to "seek actively to place Irish players into top-level cricket, by developing relationships with [especially] county cricket which will incorporate appropriate player release for Irish international duty, and feeder systems for developing Irish cricketers".[[127]](#cite_note-major_changes-131) The reorganised ICU sought closer links with the English county teams, to encourage the development of age group cricket, and to introduce a professional element into the Irish game. They also want to take the Ireland cricket team on winter tours more often.[[127]](#cite_note-major_changes-131)

In an attempt to prevent the game losing players to counties or other commitments such as jobs, it was suggested that central contracts should be introduced.[[128]](#cite_note-132) This was done in June 2009, with the first two going to [Trent Johnston](/wiki/Trent_Johnston "Trent Johnston") and [Alex Cusack](/wiki/Alex_Cusack "Alex Cusack").[[77]](#cite_note-:0-79) The number of full-time contracts was expanded to six in January 2010 with support for a further nine players; the contracts were split into three categories.[[129]](#cite_note-133)[[130]](#cite_note-134) In January 2012 the number of contracts was increased to 23, and coach Phil Simmons highlighted the process of becoming professional as an important factor in the team's success.[[131]](#cite_note-135)

## Team colours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ireland_cricket_team&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Team colours")]

In Test matches, Ireland wears cricket whites, with the optional sweater or vest with a green v-neck with the Cricket Ireland logo on the centre. The shirts feature the Cricket Ireland logo on the right breast, the manufacturer logo on the sleeve and the sponsor logo on the left breast. The fielders wear a navy blue cricket cap or a white sunhat with the Cricket Ireland logo. The batsman helmets are coloured similarly. In limited-overs cricket, Ireland wears an emerald green (in ODI) or lawn green (in T20) uniform with dark blue and white accents and feature the Cricket Ireland logo on the right breast, the sponsor logo on the centre and the manufacturer logo on the left breast. The fielders wear a dark blue baseball-style cap or sunhat.[[132]](#cite_note-136)

Dark blue, sometimes referred to as 'presidential blue', taken from both the Irish Presidential seal and the Irish quarter of the British Royal coat of arms, is historically considered a secondary national colour of Ireland, and frequently appears along with green on the kit of all-island teams e.g. hockey and rugby union.[[133]](#cite_note-137)

In ICC-regulated tournaments, the sponsor logo goes to the non-leading arm sleeve, making space for the inscription "IRELAND" written in white, on the centre section of the shirt. The current kit supplier is [Macron](/wiki/Macron_(sportswear) "Macron (sportswear)"), who signed a four-year deal in April 2023.[[134]](#cite_note-138)
